title: Isaiah 49
subtitle: The Second Servant Song: A Light for the Gentiles

sections.header: Verses 1–13 — “I Will Also Make You a Light for the Gentiles”

heb.paragraph: "I will also make you a light (ʾōr) for the Gentiles, that my salvation may reach to the ends of the earth" (v.6). THE SECOND SERVANT SONG (49:1–6). The servant speaks in first person: "Before I was born the LORD called me; from my mother’s womb he has spoken my name" (v.1). "He made my mouth like a sharpened sword" (v.2). The servant’s frustration: "I have laboured in vain; I have spent my strength for nothing" (v.4). God responds: it is too small a thing for you to restore Israel alone — "I will also make you a light for the Gentiles" (v.6). Paul and Barnabas quote v.6 in Acts 13:47 as justification for the Gentile mission. "Kings will see you and stand up, princes will see and bow down" (v.7). Simeon echoes this at Jesus’ presentation (Luke 2:30–32).

notes.note: "Before I was born the LORD called me. He made my mouth like a sharpened sword." The servant’s pre-natal calling: known before birth, named in the womb, weaponised with words.
"I have laboured in vain; I have spent my strength for nothing." The servant’s frustration. Even the Messiah experiences the feeling of futility. But: "my reward is with my God."

"It is too small a thing for you to restore Israel. I will make you a light for the Gentiles." The expansion: Israel is not enough. The servant’s mission is global. Acts 13:47: Paul quotes this as his apostolic mandate.
"Shout for joy, you heavens! The LORD comforts his people and will have compassion on his afflicted ones." The cosmic response to the servant’s commission: creation sings.

refs.note: Paul and Barnabas quote 'a light for the Gentiles' to justify their mission to non-Jews after synagogue rejection.
Simeon's Nunc Dimittis applies the light-to-nations language to the infant Jesus — the Servant embodied.
Paul's 'set apart from birth' language echoes the Servant's pre-natal commissioning.
Jeremiah's call — 'before you were born I consecrated you' — shares the Servant's election-from-the-womb pattern.

hist.context: The Second Servant Song (49:1–13) shifts from third-person introduction to first-person speech. The Servant addresses the coastlands, claiming that YHWH named him from the womb and made his mouth a sharp sword. The mission is twofold: restore Israel and become a light for the Gentiles (v.6). This dual scope resolves the earlier ambiguity — the Servant has a mission to Israel, so he cannot simply be Israel. Yet the mission is also through Israel to the nations. The song ends with a hymn of cosmic rejoicing (v.13) at the comfort YHWH provides.

sections.header: Verses 14–26 — “Can a Mother Forget Her Nursing Child?”

heb.paragraph: "Can a mother forget the baby at her breast and have no compassion (raḥămeyhā) on the child she has borne? Though she may forget, I will not forget you!" (v.15). Zion’s lament: "The LORD has forsaken me, the Lord has forgotten me" (v.14). God’s answer is the most tender metaphor in the OT: maternal love. "See, I have engraved you on the palms of my hands; your walls are ever before me" (v.16). Engraved — not written but carved. Permanent. Painful. "Lift up your eyes and look around; all your children gather and come to you" (v.18). Zion will be too small for her returned children (v.20). "Who bore me these? I was bereaved and barren; who brought these up?" (v.21). The astonished mother who lost everything now has more children than she can hold.

notes.note: "The LORD has forsaken me. Can a mother forget her nursing child? Though she may forget, I WILL NOT FORGET YOU." The "though she may forget" is stunning: God’s love exceeds even maternal love. God’s faithfulness surpasses even the strongest human bond.
"I have engraved you on the palms of my hands." Engraved, not inked. Permanent, not erasable. The Christian reading: the nail-prints in Christ’s palms.
"The children born during your bereavement will say, ‘This place is too small for us.’ Then you will say, ‘Who bore me these?’" The barren mother overwhelmed by children. The impossible reversal.

refs.note: The maternal-compassion motif recurs at the book's end: as a mother comforts, so I will comfort you.
Though father and mother forsake me, the LORD will receive me — the same surpassing-parental-love theme.
Paul insists that God has not rejected his people — the 'forsaken' lament answered by divine constancy.
Never will I leave you, never forsake you — the promise grounded in texts like Isaiah's engraved-on-palms image.

hist.context: Zion's lament — 'The LORD has forsaken me, my Lord has forgotten me' (v.14) — receives one of Scripture's most tender responses: can a mother forget her nursing child? Even if she could, YHWH will not forget. Jerusalem's name is engraved on his palms (v.16). The passage addresses the exiles' deepest fear: that covenant abandonment is permanent. The answer is categorical: your children will return, your destroyers will depart, you will be too crowded for your inhabitants. Kings and queens will bow to you (vv.22–23). The reversal of exile is total.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThis is what the Lord says: “In the time of my favor I will answer you, and in the day of salvation I will help you; I will keep you and will make you to be a covenant for the people, to restore the land and to reassign its desolate inheritances,ESVThus says the LORD: “In a time of favor I have answered you; in a day of salvation I have helped you; I will keep you and give you as a covenant to the people, to establish the land, to apportion the desolate heritages,NIVAnd now the Lord says— he who formed me in the womb to be his servant to bring Jacob back to him and gather Israel to himself, for I am honored in the eyes of the Lord and my God has been my strength—ESVAnd now the LORD says, he who formed me from the womb to be his servant, to bring Jacob back to him; and that Israel might be gathered to him— for I am honored in the eyes of the LORD, and my God has become my strength—

rec.text: ""Before I was born the LORD called me. He made my mouth like a sharpened sword." The servant’s pre-natal calling: known before birth, named in the womb, weaponised with words."

rows.label: Engraved on My Palms — 49:14–26
rows.text: "Can a MOTHER forget her child? I WILL NOT FORGET you." "I have ENGRAVED you on the PALMS of my hands."

hebtext.note: "I will also make you a light (ʾōr) for the Gentiles, that my salvation may reach to the ends of the earth" (v.6). THE SECOND SERVANT SONG (49:1–6). The servant speaks in first person: "Before I was born the LORD called me; from my mother’s womb he has spoken my name" (v.1). "He made my mouth like a sharpened sword" (v.2). Paul and Barnabas quote v.6 in Acts 13:47 as justification for the Gentile mission. "Kings will see you and stand up, princes will see and bow down" (v.7). Simeon echoes this at Jesus’ presentation (Luke 2:30–32).

Zion’s lament: "The LORD has forsaken me, the Lord has forgotten me" (v.14). God’s answer is the most tender metaphor in the OT: maternal love. "Lift up your eyes and look around; all your children gather and come to you" (v.18). Zion will be too small for her returned children (v.20). "Who bore me these? I was bereaved and barren; who brought these up?" (v.21). The astonished mother who lost everything now has more children than she can hold.

themes.note: Mercy AND Mission peak at 10: mother-love, palm-engraving, barren mother overwhelmed. Light for the Gentiles (Acts 13:47). Covenant at 9: engraved. Prophecy at 9: second Servant Song.

coaching.tip: 'It is too small a thing for you to be my servant to restore the tribes of Jacob. I will also make you a light for the Gentiles' (v. 6). The second Servant Song expands the mission beyond Israel to the world. Luke quotes this in Acts 13:47 as Paul's mandate for Gentile mission. The Servant's job description is international from the start — Israel's restoration is the means, not the end.

'Can a mother forget the baby at her breast?' (v. 15). God uses the strongest human bond — mother and nursing child — as analogy, then surpasses it: 'Though she may forget, I will not forget you.' Divine faithfulness exceeds even maternal instinct. Isaiah consistently reaches for the most powerful human experiences and then says 'God is more than that.' Every analogy points beyond itself.
